name: Mandana Misra
dates_notes: Contemporary with Śaṅkara; legendary debate may be later hagiography but textual contemporaneity is solid.

lineage_polemical.note: Maṇḍana's bhāvanā / jīva-as-locus-of-avidyā position is structurally opposed to Sureśvara's jñāna-only / Brahman-as-locus-of-avidyā stream.

Maṇḍana writes from inside Bhāṭṭa Mīmāṃsā training and engages Kumārila's jñāna-karma-samuccaya in the Brahma-Siddhi: karma is admitted as cittaśuddhi-producing but is denied to itself produce the liberating jñāna of Brahman.

core_thesis: Maṇḍana defends *advaita* from inside a Mīmāṃsaka training, and his characteristic positions follow from that intellectual location. Three theses define his account. First, on *brahman*: *brahman* is one, of the nature of self-luminous consciousness and bliss. The *Brahma-Siddhi*'s opening prose argues that worldly pleasure is fittingly construed as a delimited measure of *brahman*'s intrinsic *ānanda*, because the self is the *parapremāspada* — the locus of supreme love. Second, on *avidyā*: ignorance is *anirvacanīyā* (indeterminable, neither *sat* nor *asat*), it is plural (one per *jīva*), and it is located in the *jīvas*, not in *brahman*. Maṇḍana states this directly: *yat tu kasyāvidyeti, jīvānām iti brūmaḥ* — "as for whose ignorance it is, we say: of the *jīvas*." Third, on the path: scriptural cognition of the *mahāvākya* is necessary but does not by itself terminate ignorance. Even with *brahman*-self-identity ascertained, *sādhanāni apekṣyāṇi* — further disciplines are required — namely *tattvadarśanābhyāsa*, the repeated contemplative practice (*prasaṅkhyāna*) signaled by *mantavyo nididhyāsitavyaḥ*. These two hinges — *jīva*-locus *avidyā* and *prasaṅkhyāna* — distinguish the line carried forward through Vācaspati as *Bhāmatī* from the Sureśvara stream, which locates *avidyā* in *brahman* and treats *vākya*-cognition as immediately sufficient. Maṇḍana also inherits Bhartṛhari's *śabda*-route into *brahman*, integrating *śabda-advaita* into Vedānta proper.

engaged_works.title: Brahma-Siddhi
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored. The traditional identification of pre-conversion Maṇḍana with Sureśvara is disputed (rejected by Hiriyanna; affirmed by traditional sectarian sources).

ascription_evidence.description: Modern consensus accepts Maṇḍana's authorship of the Brahma-Siddhi.

engaged_works.summary: An independent *prakaraṇa* in four *kāṇḍas* of mixed prose and verse. *Brahma-Kāṇḍa* establishes the nature of *brahman* and the *jīva*'s relation to it; the opening prose develops the *ānanda*-argument and the formula on *avidyā* as *anirvacanīyā* whose locus is the *jīvas*. *Tarka-Kāṇḍa* defends Vedāntic non-duality on epistemological grounds. The Tarka-kāṇḍa's opening verse states the programmatic claim: perception establishes its own field but is not a *niṣeddhṛ* — it has no jurisdiction to cancel the *āgama*-teaching of unity (*naikatva āgamas tena pratyakṣeṇa virudhyate*). *Niyoga-Kāṇḍa* engages Mīmāṃsaka *niyoga*-theory on injunction and works out the relation of Vedic action to liberating knowledge. *Siddhi-Kāṇḍa* treats realization, the *prasaṅkhyāna*-method, and *jīvanmukti*. The signature doctrine — that *mahāvākya*-cognition must be prolonged into meditative *bhāvanā* before liberating realization arises — is precisely what Sureśvara's *Naiṣkarmya-Siddhi* will reject. Whether Maṇḍana is identical to the pre-conversion Sureśvara (Viśvarūpa) remains contested: the traditional sectarian sources affirm the identification; Hiriyanna and Allen Thrasher (in his monograph *The Advaita Vedānta of Brahma-Siddhi*, 1993) reject it on doctrinal grounds — the *Brahma-Siddhi* and the *Naiṣkarmya-Siddhi* diverge on exactly the points Maṇḍana defends. Standard edition: S. Kuppuswami Sastri (Madras, 1937).

key_passages.locus_long: Brahma-Siddhi, opening prose on mbs_1.1, argument that Brahman/the self is bliss by nature
key_passages.sanskrit_iast:
tasmād ātmaprakāśaprakṛṣṭātmasvabhāvam eva brahma iti yuktam /
evaṃ ca laukikānanda etasya mātrā iti yujyate, avacchedāt /
itaś cānandasvabhāva ātmā, parapremāspadatvāt /

pada_analysis.gloss: having supreme self-luminosity as intrinsic nature
because of being the locus of supreme love

samasa_vigrahas.resolution: ātmaprakāśaś ca saḥ prakṛṣṭa ātmasvabhāvaś ca yasya tat
samasa_vigrahas.note: A serial compound: self-luminosity qualifies the supreme intrinsic nature.

karaka_structure.note: Genitive relation: worldly pleasure is only a measure of this Brahman/self.
Ablative used causally: worldly pleasure is only a delimited instance.
Ablative of reason supporting the predication ānandasvabhāva ātmā.

verb_modality.note: Impersonal evaluative use: 'it is fitting / it is appropriate to construe'.

key_passages.english_close: Therefore it is fitting that Brahman is precisely of the nature of supreme self-luminosity. Thus worldly pleasure is appropriately understood as only a mere measure of this, because it is delimited. And moreover the self is of the nature of bliss, because it is the locus of supreme love.
key_passages.why_this_passage: This is not a detachable flourish from the maṅgala verse; it is Mandana's own positive construal of Brahman and self as self-luminous and bliss-natured. It fairly represents his ontology because he here explains why ordinary pleasure is derivative and why ultimate reality must be understood as intrinsic ānanda rather than mere absence of pain.
key_passages.source_edition: S. Kuppuswami Sastri (ed.), Brahma-Siddhi, Madras Government Oriental Series 4, Madras: Government Press, 1937, as represented in the GRETIL transcription (which notes a few adopted older readings).

key_passages.locus_long: Brahma-Siddhi, opening prose on mbs_1.1, argument that plurality is apprehended only under māyā and denied by śruti

other.segment: opening prose on ekatva and māyā

key_passages.sanskrit_iast:
indro māyābhiḥ pururūpa īyate iti vyaktam eva nānārūpāvagamo māyānibandhano darśitaḥ /
tathā vyakto nānātvaniṣedho neha nānāsti kiñcana iti /

pada_analysis.gloss: by māyās, by powers of appearance

pada_analysis.morphology: acc sg neut used with negative

karaka_structure.note: Subject of īyate in the cited śruti.
Instrument by which the many-formed appearance proceeds.

verb_modality.note: The cited śruti verb: 'goes / appears as many-formed'.
Finite verb inside the quoted sentence neha nānā asti kiñcana.

key_passages.english_close: "Indra goes as many-formed by māyās": thus it has been clearly shown that the apprehension of manifold forms is bound to māyā. Likewise the negation of plurality is explicit: "here there is not any plurality whatsoever."

preserved_terms.first_use_gloss: appearance-producing power; that on which plural manifestation depends

key_passages.why_this_passage: This is a fair doctrinal center of gravity for Mandana's Advaita because it states, in his own argumentative prose, both halves of the claim together: plurality is māyā-bound, and śruti's non-duality language is straightforwardly prohibitive. It is more representative than extracting only a famous mahāvākya citation because it shows how he reads those citations philosophically.
key_passages.source_edition: S. Kuppuswami Sastri (ed.), Brahma-Siddhi, Madras Government Oriental Series 4, Madras: Government Press, 1937, as represented in the GRETIL transcription (which notes a few adopted older readings).

key_passages.locus_short: BS 1.1 (avidyā neither sat nor asat; in the jīvas)
key_passages.locus_long: Brahma-Siddhi, opening prose on mbs_1.1, definition of avidyā as anirvacanīyā and statement that its locus is the jīvas
key_passages.sanskrit_iast:
nāvidyā brahmaṇaḥ svabhāvaḥ, nārthāntaraṃ, nātyantam asatī, nāpi satī /
evam eveyam avidyā māyā mithyāvabhāsa ity ucyate /
atyantāsattve khapuṣpasadṛśī na vyavahārāṅgam /
tasmād anirvacanīyā /
yat tu kasyāvidyeti, jīvānām iti brūmaḥ /

pada_analysis.gloss: in the condition of absolute non-existence

karaka_structure.note: Genitive relation denied: avidyā is not Brahman's intrinsic nature.
Locative specifying the counterfactual condition under which avidyā would be like a sky-flower.
Genitive marking the locus-possession answer to kasyā avidyā.

key_passages.english_close: Avidyā is not Brahman's intrinsic nature, not a different thing, not absolutely non-existent, and not existent either. This very avidyā is called māyā, a false appearance. If it were absolutely non-existent, like a sky-flower, it would not enter into practical transaction. Therefore it is indeterminable. And as for the question "whose avidyā is it?", we say: of the jīvas.

preserved_terms.first_use_gloss: indeterminable as either real or unreal
false appearance; an appearance not stable as ultimate fact

key_passages.why_this_passage: This is one of the most position-defining places in the work because it gives Mandana's own compressed formula for avidyā: neither Brahman's nature nor a separate real thing, neither sat nor asat, and located in the jīvas. That cluster of claims is exactly what later marks the Bhāmatī line, so choosing it is representational rather than cherry-picked.
key_passages.source_edition: S. Kuppuswami Sastri (ed.), Brahma-Siddhi, Madras Government Oriental Series 4, Madras: Government Press, 1937, as represented in the GRETIL transcription (which notes a few adopted older readings).

key_passages.locus_long: Brahma-Siddhi, mbs_1.4, opening Tarka-kāṇḍa verse on perception and scripture
key_passages.sanskrit_iast:
āhur vidhātṛ pratyakṣaṃ na niṣeddhṛ vipaścitaḥ /
naikatva āgamas tena pratyakṣeṇa virudhyate //

pada_analysis.morphology: nom sg neut with prefixed negative sandhi

samasa_vigrahas.note: Standard lexical formation: what is before the eyes / immediately presented.

karaka_structure.note: Object predicated as vidhātṛ and not niṣeddhṛ.
Instrument with reference to which contradiction is denied.

verb_modality.note: Passive: the scripture is not contradicted.

key_passages.english_close: The wise say that perception is an establisher, not a negator. Therefore the scriptural teaching of oneness is not contradicted by that perception.
key_passages.why_this_passage: This verse cleanly states Mandana's governing epistemic claim for the Tarka-kāṇḍa: perception establishes its own field but does not have jurisdiction to cancel Vedāntic non-duality. That is a representative programmatic statement rather than an isolated flourish, since the surrounding prose spends many pages unpacking exactly this asymmetry.
key_passages.source_edition: S. Kuppuswami Sastri (ed.), Brahma-Siddhi, Madras Government Oriental Series 4, Madras: Government Press, 1937, as represented in the GRETIL transcription (which notes a few adopted older readings).

key_passages.locus_long: Brahma-Siddhi, mbs_1.35-36, argument that further contemplative practice is required even after scriptural certainty
key_passages.sanskrit_iast:
tannivṛttaye 'sty anyad apekṣyam /
tac ca tattvadarśanābhyāso lokasiddhaḥ /
tathā ca mantavyo nididhyāsitavya ity ucyate /
tasmāt tannivṛttaye viniścitabrahmātmabhāvenāpi sādhanāny apekṣyāṇi /

pada_analysis.gloss: repeated practice of seeing the truth

pada_analysis.morphology: instr sg masc with indeclitic api
pada_analysis.gloss: even with the state of Brahman-self-identity being ascertained

karaka_structure.note: Dative of purpose: for the cessation of residual false appearance.
Predicate nominative identifying the 'other required factor'.
Instrumental concessive: even when Brahman-self-identity has already been ascertained.

key_passages.english_close: For the cessation of that, something else is required. And that is repeated practice of seeing the truth, something well established in ordinary experience. And thus it is said: 'it is to be reflected upon; it is to be deeply contemplated.' Therefore, even when Brahman-self-identity has been ascertained, further disciplines are required for the cessation of that residual error.

preserved_terms.first_use_gloss: repeated contemplative assimilation of the truth already understood
to be the object of sustained contemplative dwelling

key_passages.why_this_passage: This is the most direct accessible formulation, in the text on disk, of Mandana's signature prasaṅkhyāna-style position: verbal certainty is not yet enough, because further contemplative assimilation is still required. It therefore fairly represents the doctrinal hinge by which Mandana differs from jñāna-alone Advaita lines without reducing him to a caricature of 'works plus knowledge.'
key_passages.source_edition: S. Kuppuswami Sastri (ed.), Brahma-Siddhi, Madras Government Oriental Series 4, Madras: Government Press, 1937, as represented in the GRETIL transcription (which notes a few adopted older readings).
